title: Male

infobox.value: ([symbol](/wiki/Planet_symbols "Planet symbols"): [♂](/wiki/%E2%99%82 "♂")) is the [sex](/wiki/Sex "Sex") of an [organism](/wiki/Organism "Organism") that produces, or is organized to produce, the [gamete](/wiki/Gamete "Gamete") (sex cell) known as [sperm](/wiki/Sperm "Sperm"), which fuses with the larger [female](/wiki/Female "Female") gamete,[[1]](#cite_note-Lehtonen2014-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Fusco2019-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Hine2015-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) or [ovum](/wiki/Egg_cell "Egg cell"), in the process of [fertilisation](/wiki/Fertilisation "Fertilisation"). A male organism cannot [reproduce sexually](/wiki/Sexual_reproduction "Sexual reproduction") without access to at least one ovum from a female, but some organisms can reproduce both sexually and [asexually](/wiki/Asexual_reproduction "Asexual reproduction").[[5]](#cite_note-Curtis2010-5) Most male [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal"), including male humans, have a [Y chromosome](/wiki/Y_chromosome "Y chromosome"),[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) which codes for the production of larger amounts of [testosterone](/wiki/Testosterone "Testosterone") to develop [male reproductive organs](/wiki/Male_reproductive_organs "Male reproductive organs").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Sex of an organism which produces sperm

This article is about the male sex. For the capital of the Maldives, see [Malé](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9 "Malé"). For other uses, see [Male (disambiguation)](/wiki/Male_(disambiguation) "Male (disambiguation)").

"Males" redirects here. For other uses, see [Males (disambiguation)](/wiki/Males_(disambiguation) "Males (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Mars_symbol.svg/250px-Mars_symbol.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Mars_symbol.svg)

The symbol of the Roman god [Mars](/wiki/Mars_(mythology) "Mars (mythology)") (god of war) is often used to represent the male sex. It also stands for the planet [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") and is the [alchemical symbol](/wiki/Alchemical_symbol "Alchemical symbol") for [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron").

**Male** ([symbol](/wiki/Planet_symbols "Planet symbols"): [♂](/wiki/%E2%99%82 "♂")) is the [sex](/wiki/Sex "Sex") of an [organism](/wiki/Organism "Organism") that produces, or is organized to produce, the [gamete](/wiki/Gamete "Gamete") (sex cell) known as [sperm](/wiki/Sperm "Sperm"), which fuses with the larger [female](/wiki/Female "Female") gamete,[[1]](#cite_note-Lehtonen2014-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Fusco2019-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Hine2015-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) or [ovum](/wiki/Egg_cell "Egg cell"), in the process of [fertilisation](/wiki/Fertilisation "Fertilisation"). A male organism cannot [reproduce sexually](/wiki/Sexual_reproduction "Sexual reproduction") without access to at least one ovum from a female, but some organisms can reproduce both sexually and [asexually](/wiki/Asexual_reproduction "Asexual reproduction").[[5]](#cite_note-Curtis2010-5) Most male [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal"), including male humans, have a [Y chromosome](/wiki/Y_chromosome "Y chromosome"),[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) which codes for the production of larger amounts of [testosterone](/wiki/Testosterone "Testosterone") to develop [male reproductive organs](/wiki/Male_reproductive_organs "Male reproductive organs").

In humans, the word *male* can also be used to refer to [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender"), in the social sense of [gender role](/wiki/Gender_role "Gender role") or [gender identity](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity").[[8]](#cite_note-palazzani-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Overview

The existence of separate sexes has evolved independently at different times and in different [lineages](/wiki/Lineage_(evolution) "Lineage (evolution)"), an example of [convergent evolution](/wiki/Convergent_evolution "Convergent evolution").[[10]](#cite_note-Berrill-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Klymkowsky-2016-11) The repeated pattern is sexual reproduction in [isogamous](/wiki/Isogamy "Isogamy") species with two or more [mating types](/wiki/Mating_type "Mating type") with gametes of identical form and behavior (but different at the molecular level) to [anisogamous](/wiki/Anisogamy "Anisogamy") species with [gametes](/wiki/Gamete "Gamete") of male and [female](/wiki/Female "Female") types to [oogamous](/wiki/Oogamy "Oogamy") species in which the female gamete is very much larger than the male and has no ability to move. There is a good argument that this pattern was driven by the physical constraints on the mechanisms by which two gametes get together as required for [sexual reproduction](/wiki/Sexual_reproduction "Sexual reproduction").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]. But in some species males can reproduce by themselves asexually, for example via [androgenesis](/wiki/Androgenesis "Androgenesis").[[13]](#cite_note-Hedtke-2008-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Schwander-2016-14)

Accordingly, sex is defined across species by the type of gametes produced (i.e.: spermatozoa vs. ova) and differences between males and females in one lineage are not always predictive of differences in another.[[11]](#cite_note-Klymkowsky-2016-11)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Male/female dimorphism](/wiki/Sexual_dimorphism "Sexual dimorphism") between organisms or reproductive organs of different sexes is not limited to animals; male gametes are produced by [chytrids](/wiki/Chytrid "Chytrid"), [diatoms](/wiki/Diatom "Diatom") and land [plants](/wiki/Plant "Plant"), among others. In land plants, *female* and *male* designate not only the female and male gamete-producing organisms and structures but also the structures of the [sporophytes](/wiki/Sporophyte "Sporophyte") that give rise to male and female plants.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Evolution

See also: [Evolution of sexual reproduction](/wiki/Evolution_of_sexual_reproduction "Evolution of sexual reproduction") and [Sex § Evolution of sex](/wiki/Sex#Evolution_of_sex "Sex")

The [evolution of anisogamy](/wiki/Anisogamy#Evolution "Anisogamy") led to the evolution of male and female function.[[17]](#cite_note-Bachtrog-2014-17) Before the evolution of anisogamy, [mating types](/wiki/Mating_type "Mating type") in a species were [isogamous](/wiki/Isogamy "Isogamy"): the same size and both could move, catalogued only as "+" or "-" types.[[18]](#cite_note-Sawada-18): 216 In anisogamy, the mating type is called a gamete. The male gamete is smaller than the female gamete, and usually mobile.[[19]](#cite_note-Kumar-20192-19) Anisogamy remains poorly understood, as there is no fossil record of its emergence. Numerous theories exist as to why anisogamy emerged. Many share a common thread, in that larger female gametes are more likely to survive, and that smaller male gametes are more likely to find other gametes because they can travel faster. Current models often fail to account for why isogamy remains in a few species.[[20]](#cite_note-Togashi-2011-20) Anisogamy appears to have evolved multiple times from isogamy; for example, female [Volvocales](/wiki/Volvocales "Volvocales") (a type of green algae) evolved from the plus mating type.[[20]](#cite_note-Togashi-2011-20)[[18]](#cite_note-Sawada-18): 222 Although sexual evolution emerged at least 1.2 billion years ago, the lack of anisogamous fossil records make it hard to pinpoint when males evolved.[[21]](#cite_note-PB-2000-21) One theory suggests male evolved from the dominant mating type (called mating type minus).[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Symbol, etymology, and usage

### Symbol

A common [symbol](/wiki/Gender_symbol "Gender symbol") used to represent the male sex is the [Mars symbol](/wiki/Mars_symbol "Mars symbol") ♂, a circle with an arrow pointing [northeast](/wiki/Ordinal_direction "Ordinal direction"). The [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") code-point is:

:   U+2642 ♂ MALE SIGN (♂)

The symbol is identical to the planetary symbol of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"). It was first used to denote sex by [Carl Linnaeus](/wiki/Carl_Linnaeus "Carl Linnaeus") in 1751. The symbol is sometimes seen as a stylized representation of the shield and spear of the Roman god [Mars](/wiki/Mars_(mythology) "Mars (mythology)"). According to [William T. Stearn](/wiki/William_T._Stearn "William T. Stearn"), however, this derivation is "fanciful" and all the historical evidence favours "the conclusion of the French classical scholar [Claude de Saumaise](/wiki/Claude_de_Saumaise "Claude de Saumaise") (Salmasius, 1588–1683)" that it is derived from *θρ*, the contraction of a Greek name for the planet Mars, which is *Thouros*.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Etymology

Borrowed from [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *masle*, from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *masculus* ("masculine, male, worthy of a man"), diminutive of *mās* ("male person or animal, male").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Usage

In humans, the word *male* can be used in the context of [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender"), such as for gender role or gender identity of a [man](/wiki/Man "Man") or [boy](/wiki/Boy "Boy").[[8]](#cite_note-palazzani-8) For example, according to Merriam-Webster, "male" can refer to "having a gender identity that is the opposite of female".[[25]](#cite_note-25) According to the Cambridge Dictionary, "male" can mean "belonging or relating to men".[[26]](#cite_note-26)

*Male* can also refer to [a shape of connectors](/wiki/Gender_of_connectors_and_fasteners "Gender of connectors and fasteners").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Sex determination

Main article: [Sex-determination system](/wiki/Sex-determination_system "Sex-determination system")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Anterior_view_of_human_female_and_male%2C_with_labels_2.png/250px-Anterior_view_of_human_female_and_male%2C_with_labels_2.png)](/wiki/File:Anterior_view_of_human_female_and_male,_with_labels_2.png)

Photograph of an adult male human, with an adult [female](/wiki/Female "Female") for comparison. Both models have partially shaved body hair; e.g. clean-shaven pubic regions.

The sex of a particular organism may be determined by a number of factors. These may be genetic or environmental, or may naturally change during the course of an organism's life. Although most species have only two sexes (either male or female),[[10]](#cite_note-Berrill-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Klymkowsky-2016-11)[[2]](#cite_note-Fusco2019-2) [hermaphroditic](/wiki/Hermaphrodite "Hermaphrodite") animals, such as [worms](/wiki/Worm "Worm"), have both male and female reproductive organs.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Species that are divided into females and males are classified as [gonochoric](/wiki/Gonochorism "Gonochorism") in animals, as [dioecious](/wiki/Dioecy "Dioecy") in [seed plants](/wiki/Seed_plants "Seed plants")[[2]](#cite_note-Fusco2019-2) and as [dioicous](/wiki/Dioicy "Dioicy") in [cryptogams](/wiki/Cryptogam "Cryptogam").[[30]](#cite_note-ShawGoffinet-30): 82 Males can coexist with hermaphrodites, a [sexual system](/wiki/Sexual_system "Sexual system") called [androdioecy](/wiki/Androdioecy "Androdioecy"). They can also coexist with females and hermaphrodites, a sexual system called [trioecy](/wiki/Trioecy "Trioecy").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Not all species share a common [sex-determination system](/wiki/Sex-determination_system "Sex-determination system"). In most [animals](/wiki/Animal "Animal"), including [humans](/wiki/Homo_sapiens "Homo sapiens"), sex is determined [genetically](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics"); however, species such as *[Cymothoa exigua](/wiki/Cymothoa_exigua "Cymothoa exigua")* change sex depending on the number of females present in the vicinity.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Genetic determination

Most [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal"), including [humans](/wiki/Human "Human"), are genetically determined as such by the [XY sex-determination system](/wiki/XY_sex-determination_system "XY sex-determination system") where males have XY (as opposed to XX in females) [sex chromosomes](/wiki/Sex_chromosomes "Sex chromosomes"). It is also possible in a variety of species, including humans, to be [XX male](/wiki/XX_male "XX male") or have other [karyotypes](/wiki/Karyotypes "Karyotypes"). During [reproduction](/wiki/Reproduction "Reproduction"), a male can give either an X sperm or a Y sperm, while a female can only give an X egg. A Y sperm and an X egg produce a male, while an X sperm and an X egg produce a [female](/wiki/Female "Female").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The part of the Y-chromosome which is responsible for maleness is the sex-determining region of the Y-chromosome, the [SRY](/wiki/SRY "SRY").[[34]](#cite_note-34) The SRY activates [Sox9](/wiki/Sox9 "Sox9"), which forms feedforward loops with [FGF9](/wiki/FGF9 "FGF9") and [PGD2](/wiki/PGD2 "PGD2") in the [gonads](/wiki/Gonad "Gonad"), allowing the levels of these genes to stay high enough in order to cause male development;[[35]](#cite_note-Moniot-35) for example, Fgf9 is responsible for development of the [spermatic cords](/wiki/Spermatic_cord "Spermatic cord") and the multiplication of [Sertoli cells](/wiki/Sertoli_cell "Sertoli cell"), both of which are crucial to male sexual development.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The [ZW sex-determination system](/wiki/ZW_sex-determination_system "ZW sex-determination system"), where males have ZZ (as opposed to ZW in females) sex chromosomes, may be found in [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird") and some [insects](/wiki/Insect "Insect") (mostly [butterflies and moths](/wiki/Lepidoptera "Lepidoptera")) and other organisms. Members of the insect order [Hymenoptera](/wiki/Hymenoptera "Hymenoptera"), such as [ants](/wiki/Ant "Ant") and [bees](/wiki/Bee "Bee"), are often determined by [haplodiploidy](/wiki/Haplodiploidy "Haplodiploidy"),[[17]](#cite_note-Bachtrog-2014-17) where most males are [haploid](/wiki/Haploid "Haploid") and females and some sterile males are [diploid](/wiki/Diploid "Diploid"). However, fertile diploid males may still appear in some species, such as *[Cataglyphis cursor](/wiki/Cataglyphis_cursor "Cataglyphis cursor")*.[[37]](#cite_note-dip-37)

### Environmental determination

In some species of reptiles, such as [alligators](/wiki/Alligator "Alligator"), sex is determined by the temperature at which the egg is incubated. Other species, such as some [snails](/wiki/Snail "Snail"), practice sex change: adults start out male, then become female.[[38]](#cite_note-38) In tropical [clown fish](/wiki/Clown_fish "Clown fish"), the dominant individual in a group becomes female while the other ones are male.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Secondary sex characteristics

Main article: [Secondary sex characteristic](/wiki/Secondary_sex_characteristic "Secondary sex characteristic")

Male animals have evolved to use secondary sex characteristics as a way of displaying traits that signify their [fitness](/wiki/Fitness_(biology) "Fitness (biology)"). [Sexual selection](/wiki/Sexual_selection "Sexual selection") is believed to be the driving force behind the development of these characteristics. Differences in physical size and the ability to fulfill the requirements of sexual selection have contributed significantly to the outcome of secondary sex characteristics in each species.[[40]](#cite_note-Campbell-40)

In many species, males differ from females in more ways than just the production of sperm. For example, in some insects and fish, the male is smaller than the female. In seed plants, the [sporophyte sex organ](/wiki/Flower "Flower") of a single organism includes both the male and female parts.

In mammals, including humans, males are typically larger than females. This is often attributed to the need for male mammals to be physically stronger and more competitive in order to win mating opportunities. In humans specifically, males have more body hair and muscle mass than females.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[42]](#cite_note-42)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Birds often exhibit colorful [plumage](/wiki/Plumage "Plumage") that attracts females.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] This is true for many species of birds where the male displays more vibrant colors than the female, making them more noticeable to potential mates. These characteristics have evolved over time as a result of sexual selection, as males who exhibited these traits were more successful in attracting mates and passing on their genes.
